back_translation: And one day, Jonathan son of Saul said to the young man who bore his armor, 'Come, let us cross over to the Philistine outpost on the other side.' But he did not tell his father.
And Saul was staying at the edge of Gibeah, under the pomegranate tree at Migron, and the people who were with him numbered about six hundred men.
Including Ahijah, son of Ahitub, brother of Ichabod, son of Phinehas, son of Eli, priest of the LORD at Shiloh — the one wearing the ephod. And the people did not know that Jonathan had gone.

And between the passes by which Jonathan sought to cross over to the Philistine outpost, there was a sharp cliff on one side and a sharp cliff on the other side; and the name of one was Bozez and the name of the other was Seneh.
One cliff stood to the north opposite Michmash, and the other to the south opposite Geba.

And Jonathan said to the young man who bore his armor, 'Come, let us cross over to the outpost of these uncircumcised men. Perhaps the LORD will work on our behalf, for there is no hindrance for the LORD from saving by many or by few.'
And his armor-bearer said to him, 'Do all that is in your heart. Go on; here I am with you, according to your heart.'
And Jonathan said, 'Behold, we will cross over to those men and we will reveal ourselves to them.'
If they say thus to us, 'Wait until we come to you,' then we will stay where we are and not go up to them.
But if they say thus, 'Come up to us,' then we will go up, for this will be the sign to us that the LORD has given them into our hand.
And the two of them revealed themselves to the Philistine outpost, and the Philistines said, 'Look, the Hebrews are coming out of the holes where they hid themselves.'

And the men of the outpost answered Jonathan and his armor-bearer and said, 'Come up to us, and we will teach you a lesson.' And Jonathan said to his armor-bearer, 'Come up after me, for the LORD has given them into the hand of Israel.'
And Jonathan went up on his hands and on his feet, with his armor-bearer following him; and the Philistines fell before Jonathan, and his armor-bearer killed them after him.
And in that first assault that Jonathan and his armor-bearer struck, they killed about twenty men, in about half a furrow of a yoke of field.
And there was panic in the camp, in the field, and among all the people; the outpost and the raiders trembled too. And the earth shook, and it became a panic of God.
And the watchmen of Saul at Gibeah of Benjamin saw, and behold, the multitude was melting and going in every direction.
And Saul said to the people who were with him, 'Count the troops and see who has gone from us.' And they counted, and behold, Jonathan and his armor-bearer were not there.
And Saul said to Ahijah, 'Bring near the ark of God.' For the ark of God was on that day with the children of Israel.
And it happened that while Saul was speaking to the priest, the multitude in the Philistine camp went on and increased. And Saul said to the priest, 'Withdraw your hand.'
And Saul and all the people who were with him rallied and they came to the battle, and behold, the sword of each man was against his fellow — a very great confusion.
And the Hebrews who had been with the Philistines as before, who had gone up with them in the camp around, even they turned to be with Israel who were with Saul and Jonathan.
And all the men of Israel who had hidden themselves in the hill country of Ephraim heard that the Philistines were fleeing, and they also pursued them in the battle.
And the LORD saved Israel on that day, and the battle passed beyond Beth-aven.

And the men of Israel were hard-pressed on that day, for Saul had bound the people with an oath, saying, 'Cursed be the man who eats food until evening, until I have avenged myself on my enemies.' And none of the people tasted food.
And all the people came into the forest, and there was honey on the surface of the field.
And the people entered the forest, and behold, the honey was flowing, but not one put his hand to his mouth, for the people feared the oath.

But Jonathan had not heard when his father bound the people with the oath. So he reached out the end of the staff that was in his hand and dipped it in the honeycomb, and put his hand to his mouth, and his eyes brightened.
And one of the people answered and said, 'Your father bound the people with a solemn oath, saying, Cursed be the man who eats food today.' And the people were faint.
And Jonathan said, 'My father has troubled the land. Look how my eyes have brightened because I tasted a little of this honey.'
How much more if the people had eaten freely today of the plunder of their enemies that they found! For now the slaughter among the Philistines has not been great.
And on that day they struck the Philistines from Michmash to Aijalon, and the people were very faint.
And the people rushed to the plunder, and they took sheep and oxen and calves, and slaughtered them on the ground; and the people ate with the blood.
And they told Saul, saying, 'Behold, the people are sinning against the LORD by eating with the blood.' And he said, 'You have broken faith. Roll a great stone to me today.'

And Saul said, 'Disperse among the people and say to them, Each man bring me his ox and each man his sheep, and slaughter them here and eat — and do not sin against the LORD by eating with the blood.' And all the people brought, each man his ox in his hand, that night, and they slaughtered there.
And Saul built an altar to the LORD; this was the first altar he built to the LORD.

And Saul said, 'Let us go down after the Philistines by night and plunder them until the light of morning, and let us not leave a man among them.' And they said, 'Do all that is good in your eyes.' But the priest said, 'Let us draw near here to God.'
And Saul inquired of God, 'Shall I go down after the Philistines? Will You give them into the hand of Israel?' But He did not answer him on that day.
And Saul said, 'Come here, all leaders of the people, and consider and see in what this sin has been today.'
For as the LORD lives, who saves Israel — even if it is in Jonathan my son, he shall surely die.' But none of all the people answered him.
And he said to all Israel, 'You shall be on one side, and I and Jonathan my son will be on one side.' And the people said to Saul, 'Do what is good in your eyes.'
And Saul said to the LORD God of Israel, 'Give a perfect answer.' And Jonathan and Saul were taken, and the people went out free.
And Saul said, 'Cast lots between me and Jonathan my son.' And Jonathan was taken.
And Saul said to Jonathan, 'Tell me what you have done.' And Jonathan told him and said, 'I tasted a little honey with the end of the staff that was in my hand. Behold, I will die.'
And Saul said, 'May God do thus, and more also, if you, Jonathan, shall not surely die.'

But the people said to Saul, 'Shall Jonathan die — who has done this great deliverance in Israel? Far be it! As the LORD lives, not a hair of his head shall fall to the ground, for with God he has worked today.' And the people ransomed Jonathan, and he did not die.
And Saul went up from following the Philistines, and the Philistines went to their place.

And Saul took the kingdom over Israel, and he fought all around against all his enemies — against Moab and against the children of Ammon and against Edom and against the kings of Zobah and against the Philistines. And wherever he turned, he routed them.
And he acted valiantly, and struck Amalek, and saved Israel from the hand of those who plundered it.
And the sons of Saul were Jonathan and Ishvi and Malchishua, and the names of his two daughters: the name of the firstborn was Merab and the name of the younger was Michal.
And the name of Saul's wife was Ahinoam daughter of Ahimaaz, and the name of the commander of his army was Abner son of Ner, Saul's uncle.
And Kish, Saul's father, and Ner, Abner's father, were sons of Abiel.
And the war was strong against the Philistines all the days of Saul; and whenever Saul saw any mighty man or any son of valor, he gathered him to himself.
